mappings.rationaleAI: Comprehensive agricultural science covering soil management (C.1), plant cultivation (C.2), pest control (C.5), animal husbandry and breeding (D.1), livestock disease control (D.3), farm labour and machinery (B.2), farming in relation to weather and pollution (B.4), and the history of agriculture (A).

Human relationship with food covering sensory perception, diet, nutrition, food poisoning, agricultural technology, and feeding nine billion people connects to food processing (F), food preservation (G), quality control (H), and new food sources and product development (I).
Biochemical principles of nutrition covering macronutrients, vitamins, and metabolic processes relate to evaluation of food quality (H.1) and control of food quality (H.2).

Plant biology covering evolution, diversity, pollination, and the role of cereal crops, fruit, and vegetables in human civilization connects to crop farming (C), plant propagation (C.2), and major crops (C.6).

Ecological principles of population dynamics, interspecies interactions, and environmental policy connect to farming in relation to pollution and environmental concerns (B.4) and pest and disease control through ecological understanding (C.5).
Forest ecosystem services including timber production and forestry management directly address forest crops (C.6.c).
Microbial communities in plants and animals affecting growth and disease protection connect to pest and disease control in crops (C.5) and livestock disease control (D.3).
Microorganisms including bacteria and fungi with applications in agriculture and biotechnology connect to pest and disease control (C.5) and food processing through fermentation (F).
Fungi as agents of crop disease causing historical famines directly address control of pests and disease organisms in crop farming (C.5).
Insect diversity including herbivores and parasites, and the impact of pesticides on populations, directly addresses control of pests and disease organisms (C.5).
Genetic variation, hereditary mechanisms, and molecular biology connect to animal breeding (D.1) and utilization of new food sources through genetic modification (I.2).
Selective breeding of crops, horses, and cattle as practical applications of heredity directly addresses animal breeding (D.1).
Mammalian biology, diversity, and reproductive strategies provide scientific foundations for understanding major livestock animals (D.2).
Marine organism diversity, overfishing impacts, and aquaculture as food production technology directly address technology of fishing (E).
Bacteria as disease agents and their ecological functions in element recycling connect to pest and disease control in crops (C.5) and livestock disease (D.3).
Pathogen transmission, vaccination strategies, and disease management connect to control of pests and disease organisms in crops (C.5) and livestock disease and pest control (D.3).
Climate change effects on ecological systems and agricultural production connect to farming in relation to weather and environmental concerns (B.4).
Sustainable production and consumption patterns, material flows, and environmental limits connect to farming in relation to environmental concerns (B.4).
Invasive species biology and ecological impacts including biodiversity losses connect to control of pests and disease organisms affecting crops (C.5).
